simulation_parameters.noise_description: Each response has 7.5% probability of uniform random reassignment across {0,1,2,3}

weakness_avoider.label: Weakness-Avoider
weakness_avoider.description: Chooses lowest-growth option frequently; avoids discomfort

growth_intentional.label: Growth-Intentional
growth_intentional.description: Chooses growth-oriented but structured options deliberately

overconfident_static.label: Overconfident-Static
overconfident_static.description: Chooses high-agency, low-reflection options; appears high-scoring but non-adaptive

feedback_seeker.label: Feedback-Seeker
feedback_seeker.description: Chooses feedback-heavy answers; relies on external input

reactive_improver.label: Reactive-Improver
reactive_improver.description: Improves only after failure; moderate growth patterns

strategic_learner.label: Strategic-Learner
strategic_learner.description: Balanced growth + systems thinking; highest effective Drive

population_statistics.note: All 4 fields share identical question structure and scoring; results are field-invariant under this simulation model

item_level_analysis.note: All 4 items within a field share identical response model per archetype, producing symmetric item statistics. In real data, items will diverge based on content-specific difficulty.

per_item_template.item_discrimination_interpretation: Moderate-to-good; adequate for a 4-item baseline proxy

internal_consistency.interpretation: Moderate reliability. Acceptable for a 4-item behavioural baseline proxy. Would improve with 6+ items. True Drive is computed longitudinally, so MC alpha is a lower bound.

archetype_separability.interpretation: Strong overall separability. Extreme archetypes (WA vs OS) show d>2.0. Adjacent archetypes (FS↔RI, GI↔SL) show moderate overlap — expected and acceptable. The Overconfident-Static archetype scores highest on MC but would score lowest on longitudinal Drive (no actual improvement), validating the two-stage design.

results.archetype_separability: Reduced but still meaningful. Extreme archetypes still distinguishable (d > 1.5). Adjacent archetypes merge.
results.verdict: DEGRADED BUT FUNCTIONAL — noise above 15% begins to compress discrimination

results.archetype_separability: Low internal discrimination; most candidates cluster in 25–45 band. Growth-oriented minority still distinguishable.
results.verdict: COMPRESSED BUT VALID — floor risk approaches threshold; population lacks variance

results.archetype_separability: Moderate discrimination; ceiling risk elevated but within tolerance. Low-Drive minority clearly distinguishable.
results.verdict: STABLE — ceiling risk controlled; distribution shifted but functional

comparison_summary.stability_verdict: Mean is robust across perturbations (range 37.6–59.8). Alpha degrades under noise but remains > 0.49. No scenario triggers ceiling or floor threshold violations.

limitations: Synthetic simulation assumes item-level independence — real responses exhibit inter-item correlation from shared context
Archetype response profiles are expert-designed heuristics, not empirically derived
All fields receive identical statistical treatment; real data may show field-specific item difficulty
4-item scale inherently limits alpha; this is a known trade-off for assessment length
Positional scoring (0-3) treats all options as equidistant — IRT modeling would capture non-linear discrimination
Noise model assumes uniform random reassignment; real noise patterns may be systematically biased
N=1000 synthetic is sufficient for distributional analysis but cannot replace empirical item calibration
verdict_rationale: Drive MC questions demonstrate adequate psychometric properties for a 4-item behavioural baseline proxy. Meaningful archetype differentiation (d > 0.4 for all non-adjacent pairs), no ceiling/floor violations, and acceptable internal consistency (α = 0.624). The Overconfident-Static archetype's high MC score validates the two-stage design (MC baseline + longitudinal velocity). Recommend empirical IRT calibration after N ≥ 500 real responses.
